article_title: Caddy handles reverse proxying so well that I ditched my entire toolchain
core_thesis: Caddy's modular architecture, automatic TLS certificate generation via Let's Encrypt, and simple single-file configuration make it a superior reverse proxy for self-hosting, allowing users to replace complex toolchains with a streamlined, customizable setup that scales with their homelab.
detailed_summary: The article argues that Caddy is an ideal reverse proxy for self-hosted services due to its ease of use, modularity, and automatic HTTPS. The author explains that Caddy's modular design allows users to add only the features they need, such as the Cloudflare DNS module for TLS challenges, without bloat. They provide concrete steps for building custom Caddy images using xcaddy and Docker, and show how to configure a Caddyfile to reverse proxy services with minimal effort. The article also includes a comparison to other tools like Traefik and Nginx, with some commenters preferring Traefik or Pangolin, but the author maintains that Caddy's simplicity and flexibility are key advantages. The author emphasizes that Caddy's single-file management and automatic certificate renewal reduce administrative overhead, making it ideal for growing homelabs.

entities.name: Caddy
entities.role: reverse proxy and web server with automatic TLS and modular design

entities.name: Cloudflare
entities.role: DNS provider used for DNS-01 challenges to obtain Let's Encrypt certificates

entities.name: Let's Encrypt
entities.role: certificate authority providing free TLS certificates

entities.name: xcaddy
entities.role: tool for building custom Caddy binaries with additional modules

entities.name: Docker
entities.role: containerization platform used to deploy custom Caddy images

entities.name: Traefik
entities.role: alternative reverse proxy mentioned in comments as simpler to deploy

entities.name: Pangolin
entities.role: alternative reverse proxy with UI and VPN-like connectivity, mentioned in comments

key_data_points: Caddy automatically generates TLS certificates via Let's Encrypt, requiring only a DNS challenge module (e.g., Cloudflare) for domains behind a home network. (🟢 Relevant to your homelab: simplifies HTTPS setup for all self-hosted services.)

Custom Caddy images are built using xcaddy with a command like: xcaddy build --with github.com/caddy-dns/cloudflare --with github.com/mholt/caddy-dynamicdns. (🟢 Relevant to your DevOps: provides a reproducible method to extend Caddy's functionality.)
A minimal Caddyfile entry to reverse proxy a service is: example.com { reverse_proxy http://<internal-ip>:<port> }. (🟢 Relevant to your homelab: shows how quickly you can expose services.)
The Cloudflare API token is set via environment variable: export CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN='your-api-token'. (🟢 Relevant to your homelab: essential for DNS-01 challenges behind NAT.)

Commenters note that Traefik is simpler to deploy ('just deploy and voila it works'), and Pangolin adds a UI and VPN-like connectivity. (🟢 Relevant to your decision-making: consider alternatives if you prefer a GUI or zero-config approach.)
methodology_steps: Install Caddy or use a prebuilt Proxmox LXC. For custom modules, build a new binary with xcaddy: xcaddy build --with <module-path>.
Create a Dockerfile that uses caddy:builder as a base, runs caddy-builder with desired modules, then copies the binary to a fresh caddy:latest image.
In docker-compose.yml, use 'build: .' instead of 'image:' to build the custom image.
Alternatively, use Caddy's web configurator to select modules and download a prebuilt binary. Install with: sudo curl -o /usr/bin/caddy <url> && sudo chmod 755 /usr/bin/caddy.
Set the Cloudflare API token as an environment variable: export CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN='your-api-token'.
Create a Caddyfile with entries like: example.com { reverse_proxy http://<internal-ip>:<port> }.
Run Caddy with: caddy run --config /etc/caddy/Caddyfile --adapter caddyfile.
conclusions: Caddy's modular design and automatic TLS make it a powerful yet simple reverse proxy for self-hosting, reducing the need for multiple tools. The author successfully replaced their entire toolchain with Caddy, citing ease of management and scalability.

While alternatives like Traefik and Pangolin exist, Caddy's single-file configuration and straightforward module system appeal to users who prefer control and minimal overhead. The article's comments show mixed opinions, but the author's experience highlights Caddy's strengths for homelab enthusiasts.

For homelab users, Caddy's Cloudflare DNS module is essential for obtaining TLS certificates behind a home network, and the ability to rebuild images with xcaddy allows for tailored setups without bloat.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your homelab and DevOps interests, providing a practical guide to replacing a complex reverse proxy toolchain with Caddy. You can directly apply the steps to set up Caddy in your own homelab, leveraging its automatic TLS and modularity to expose services securely. The article also touches on alternative tools like Traefik and Pangolin, which you may want to evaluate based on your preference for simplicity vs. control. As a programmer, the Dockerfile and xcaddy examples offer a reproducible, code-driven approach to infrastructure.
extra_advice: Consider starting with Caddy's web configurator to generate a custom binary with only the modules you need (e.g., Cloudflare, dynamic DNS). This avoids the complexity of building from source initially. Once comfortable, you can automate builds with a CI pipeline using xcaddy and Docker, ensuring your reverse proxy stays up-to-date with minimal effort.
